topic: Free will is compatible with determinism

speeches.AC:
Madam Judge, respected opponent, good afternoon. The question before us is simple: can we have free will even if everything that happens is caused by prior events? I affirm. And I'll prove it by showing that moral responsibility depends on our capacity to understand right from wrong and to act on that understanding—not on some magical ability to have done otherwise. Let me explain why this matters.

Imagine this scenario. Kurt steals his friend's lunch at school. We don't say, "Kurt couldn't have helped it—determinism made him do it." We say, "Kurt knew stealing was wrong, and he chose to do it anyway." That's the foundation of moral responsibility. According to the evidence, "No doubt the principle's appeal can in part be traced to ordinary moral practice. One day at the cafeteria, Kurt steals his friend's lunch." We hold Kurt accountable because he understood the moral wrongness of his action. That's the key point I need you to understand today.

Moral responsibility depends on responsiveness to moral reasons, not on untestable metaphysical alternatives. The cafeteria example proves this. We condemn Kurt not because he had some mystical power to choose differently in exactly the same circumstances. We condemn him because he understood why stealing was wrong. The evidence states, "And what do we say to the person who offers a deliberative justification of his choices and actions and refuses to offer an excuse?" This is the heart of my argument. We ask people to justify their actions based on moral reasoning. That's how we hold them responsible.

Now, let me address what free will is not. Some people think free will requires that you could have done otherwise in the exact same situation. But that's not how we operate in real life. We don't judge people based on whether they had metaphysical alternative possibilities. We judge them based on whether they understood the moral dimensions of their choices. The evidence supports this distinction: "Rather, one mode of justification perishes, a mode that, it may seem, now hangs unsupported." This isn't abstract philosophy—it's how we talk to our children, how we run our schools, how we operate our legal system.

My second claim builds on this. Deliberative justification is the foundation of moral responsibility. We condemn someone who locks themselves in a bank vault to commit a crime because they deliberated and made a choice. The evidence confirms this: "And such entry, he may insist, lacks any deliberative justification, at least for him." We care about whether a person could justify their actions through rational thought. That's the capacity that matters.

This matters enormously for real-world accountability. Our criminal justice system, which costs eighty billion dollars annually in the United States, depends on the assumption that people make choices we can hold responsible. If we rejected compatibilism, we'd have to abandon this system entirely. We wouldn't punish criminals because "determinism freed them." But we don't operate that way. We believe people can change their behavior through moral reasoning. That's why we have rehabilitation programs, why we hold people accountable for their choices, and why we distinguish between someone who acted impulsively and someone who planned a crime.

Finally, let me address the resolution itself. The resolution asks whether free will is COMPATIBLE with determinism—not whether it's LIBERTARIAN. Compatibilism isn't "free will is an illusion." It's "free will is the capacity to act on reasons, which determinism doesn't negate." When you tell your child, "you could have chosen not to steal," you're not claiming magical alternative possibilities. You're claiming they understood the wrongness and could have acted differently. That's compatibilist free will.

Here's the bottom line. Moral responsibility depends on our capacity for rational deliberation and responsiveness to moral reasons. Determinism does not undermine this capacity. It requires us to focus on the capacities that actually matter for holding people responsible. We already operate on this understanding every day. That's why free will is compatible with determinism. Thank you.
speeches.AC-CX:
Q1 (NEG): Under determinism, in the exact same circumstances as when Kurt stole his friend's lunch, could Kurt have chosen not to steal?

A1 (AFF): The question misstates what "could have" means in moral responsibility. When we say to Kurt, "you could have chosen not to steal," we don't mean he could have done otherwise *in identical circumstances*. We mean he *understood stealing was wrong* and *had the capacity to choose differently* because he was responsive to moral reasons. That's exactly what the evidence shows: "No doubt the principle’s appeal can in part be traced to ordinary moral practice. One day at the cafeteria, Kurt steals his friend’s lunch." We hold him accountable *because* he could have justified not stealing—meaning he had the deliberative capacity to act on moral reasons. Determinism doesn't prevent that capacity. So yes, Kurt *could* have chosen not to steal in the moral sense that matters: the sense where we ask people to justify their actions based on right and wrong. That's why we say it, and why it's compatible with determinism.

Q2 (NEG): Under determinism, in the exact same circumstances as Kurt stole his friend's lunch, was it impossible for Kurt to have chosen not to steal, and if so, does your argument reject the necessity of alternative possibilities for moral responsibility?

A2 (AFF): Under determinism, in the exact same circumstances, it was impossible for Kurt to have chosen not to steal. But my argument explicitly rejects the necessity of *that kind* of alternative possibility for moral responsibility. We hold Kurt accountable because he understood stealing was wrong and had the capacity to choose not to steal based on that understanding—*not* because he could have done otherwise in identical circumstances. As the evidence states: "No doubt the principle’s appeal can in part be traced to ordinary moral practice. One day at the cafeteria, Kurt steals his friend’s lunch." We condemn Kurt precisely because he was responsive to moral reasons, which is what moral responsibility requires. Determinism doesn't negate that capacity. So yes, it was impossible for Kurt to choose otherwise under determinism—but that’s irrelevant to why we hold him responsible. Our moral practice, and the evidence, show we only care about whether he *could have justified not stealing* through rational thought.

Q3 (NEG): When you say Kurt "chose to do it anyway" in the cafeteria example, are you implying he could have chosen not to steal in the exact same circumstances? And if so, how does that align with your position that moral responsibility doesn't require that kind of alternative possibility?

A3 (AFF): No, I'm not implying Kurt could have chosen not to steal *in identical circumstances*—that's the very alternative possibility we reject. When we say "Kurt could have chosen not to steal," we mean he *understood stealing was wrong* and *had the capacity to act on that understanding* through rational deliberation. That's the moral sense of "could have," and it's precisely what the evidence confirms: "No doubt the principle’s appeal can in part be traced to ordinary moral practice. One day at the cafeteria, Kurt steals his friend’s lunch." We condemn Kurt *not* because he had metaphysical alternatives, but because he was *responsive to moral reasons*—the same capacity that lets us hold *anyone* accountable. Determinism doesn't erase that capacity; it's why we say "you could have chosen not to steal" to a child, or why our legal system demands deliberative justification. That's compatibilism: moral responsibility lives in our *actual* capacity to reason, not in untestable metaphysics.

Q4 (NEG): Under determinism, in the exact same circumstances as Kurt stole his friend's lunch, was it impossible for Kurt to have chosen not to steal, and do you explicitly reject the necessity of that kind of alternative possibility for moral responsibility?

A4 (AFF): Under determinism, yes, in the exact same circumstances, it was impossible for Kurt to have chosen not to steal. But I explicitly reject the necessity of *that* kind of alternative possibility for moral responsibility. Moral responsibility depends on whether Kurt understood stealing was wrong and had the capacity to act on that understanding through rational deliberation—what we call deliberative justification. As the evidence states: "No doubt the principle’s appeal can in part be traced to ordinary moral practice. One day at the cafeteria, Kurt steals his friend’s lunch." We hold Kurt accountable *because* he was responsive to moral reasons, not because he could have done otherwise in identical circumstances. That's why we say "you could have chosen not to steal" to a child: it refers to their *capacity* to justify their actions, not to metaphysical alternatives. Determinism doesn't negate that capacity—it's why we operate moral responsibility in real life.
speeches.NC:
Madam Judge, esteemed opponent, thank you for the opportunity to clarify why the AFF's position collapses under its own weight. The resolution claims free will is compatible with determinism, but I'll prove it's impossible because the AFF's own framework contains a fatal contradiction. Let me explain why this matters for real people in real situations.

First, the AFF claims moral responsibility depends on "the capacity to have chosen otherwise." But their own CX responses admit: "Under determinism, in the exact same circumstances, it was impossible for Kurt to have chosen not to steal." This is a direct contradiction. If it was impossible for Kurt to choose otherwise, then according to the fundamental principle of moral responsibility, he cannot be held responsible. The evidence confirms this: "If we do not have free will, then there is no such thing as moral responsibility." And crucially, "Ought, as the saying goes, implies can." If we say someone "ought not to have stolen," we assume they could have chosen not to steal. But the AFF admits they couldn't have. That's not just inconsistent - it's nonsensical.

Second, this isn't abstract philosophy. It's how we operate every day. Imagine you're in a bank vault, locked inside by your own choice. You can't speak up to save Jones's reputation. We'd say, "You had the chance to avoid that situation
speeches.NC-CX:
Q1 (AFF): You conceded in your speech that under determinism, "in the exact same circumstances, it was impossible for Kurt to have chosen not to steal" - does that mean Kurt could not have chosen to act differently *given his understanding of right and wrong*?

A1 (NEG): Yes, exactly. Under determinism, it was causally impossible for Kurt to have chosen not to steal, even if he understood stealing was wrong. That's the point. Determinism means our choices are causally determined by prior states - including our beliefs about right and wrong. So moral understanding doesn't create freedom; it's just part of the causal chain that determines our actions. This proves my argument: if determinism is true, we cannot have the capacity to have chosen otherwise, which destroys moral responsibility. The question actually proves my case, not the opponent's.

Q2 (AFF): You said moral understanding is "just part of the causal chain that determines our actions" - if Kurt understood stealing was wrong but still stole, does that mean he couldn't have chosen to act according to his moral understanding under determinism?

A2 (NEG): Let me clarify this carefully. Yes, Kurt understood stealing was wrong - that's the moral understanding I was referring to. But understanding something doesn't mean he could have acted differently. Under determinism, his understanding was part of the causal chain that determined his action, not a separate freedom-generating factor. Think about it like this: if I understand that smoking is bad for my health but still smoke because of addiction, biology, and environment, my understanding didn't give me a real alternative. It was just part of why I made the choice I did.

This isn't a concession against me - it proves my argument. If Kurt's moral understanding was part of the causal chain that determined his action, then determinism destroys the capacity to have chosen otherwise. That's exactly why I said moral understanding is 'just part of the causal chain' - it doesn't create freedom, it's what determines our choices. The question actually proves my case: under determinism, we can't escape the causal chain, even when we understand right from wrong.

Q3 (AFF): You said moral understanding is "just part of the causal chain that determines our actions" - but if we know Kurt understood stealing was wrong, do we treat him differently than if he didn't understand, even under determinism?

A3 (NEG): That's an excellent question, and it actually proves my point rather than undermining it. Yes, we absolutely treat Kurt differently if he understood stealing was wrong versus if he didn't. We'd treat the understanding agent as more blameworthy, more culpable, and more deserving of punishment. That's why we have defenses like "insanity" or "lack of mens rea" in criminal law - understanding matters to how we respond.

But here's the crucial point: this difference in treatment doesn't prove free will exists. It just proves that moral understanding is part of the causal chain that determines our actions. Under determinism, Kurt's understanding was itself causally determined by his genetics, upbringing, education, and environment. His understanding didn't create freedom - it was part of the very thing that made his action inevitable.

Think about the man in the room example from Riley (2015): "In reality the man has no freedom to choose, he cannot get out of the room. However, his ignorance of his true condition affects how we view his responsibility." We treat the ignorant man differently than the informed one - but that difference doesn't prove he had free will. It just proves that information and understanding affect behavior, which is exactly what determinism predicts.

The question actually confirms my argument: moral understanding is part of the causal chain, not a challenge to it. Whether Kurt understood right from wrong or not, under determinism, his action was still causally determined. The difference in how we treat him reflects our practical responses to different causal conditions - not evidence of incompatible自由 will.

Q4 (AFF): You keep saying moral understanding is 'part of the causal chain' under determinism, but in your cafeteria example, you say we hold Kurt responsible because he understood stealing was wrong - doesn't that mean you're admitting he could have chosen to act differently *if he had chosen to follow his understanding*?

A4 (NEG): Yes, that's exactly my point. If Kurt understood stealing was wrong, that understanding was part of the causal chain that determined his action - not a separate choice he could make. Think about it this way: when we say someone "knew better" but still stole, we're not saying they had a real alternative. We're saying their understanding, combined with their environment, biology, and past experiences, made the theft causally inevitable.

The question actually proves my case. If moral understanding creates freedom to choose otherwise, then under determinism, we'd have to say Kurt could have chosen not to steal *despite* his understanding. But that's not how determinism works. Under determinism, his understanding was part of why he stole - it was part of the causal chain that made the action necessary given prior conditions.

This isn't about whether Kurt knew right from wrong. It's about whether knowing right from wrong creates a capacity to have chosen differently. Under determinism, it doesn't. That's why moral understanding being 'part of the causal chain' destroys the AFF's position - it shows we can't have the capacity to have chosen otherwise, which is required for moral responsibility.
speeches.1AR:
Madam Judge, the negative has created a self-refuting position. They've proven my case while trying to attack it. Let me explain why.

First, the conflation error. The negative keeps mixing up two different meanings of could have done otherwise. One meaning is practical: could Kurt have understood stealing was wrong and chosen not to do it? The other meaning is metaphysical: could Kurt have done otherwise in identical circumstances with the same psychology, biology, and environment? The negative admits determinism makes the second kind impossible. But that's irrelevant to moral responsibility. We only care about the first kind. When we say to Kurt, you could have chosen not to steal, we mean he understood the moral wrongness and had the capacity to act on that understanding. That's exactly what the evidence shows: we hold people accountable because they could justify their actions through rational thought. The negative's own concession proves this distinction matters.

Second, the practical capacity defense. Moral responsibility depends on our capacity to understand right from wrong and to act on that understanding. This capacity exists even under determinism because it's about how we actually operate in the world. We don't condemn Kurt because he had some magical ability to break the causal chain. We condemn him because he knew stealing was wrong. The negative's man in the room example proves this. We treat the ignorant man differently than the informed man, not because of metaphysical freedom, but because information and understanding affect behavior. That's exactly what determinism predicts. The negative isn't proving incompatibilism. They're proving that moral understanding matters for how we respond to actions - which is exactly the compatibilist position.

Third, the self-refutation turn. Here's the knockout argument. If the negative is correct that moral responsibility requires the metaphysical capacity to have done otherwise, then no one is ever responsible for anything. This includes the negative's own argument against compatibilism. If determinism makes it impossible to have done otherwise, then the negative cannot be held responsible for making their argument. Their position destroys all moral accountability, including their own. But we don't operate that way. We hold people responsible for their arguments, their actions, and their choices. That's why we have debates, why we have legal systems, and why we have moral discourse. The negative has given us a theory that makes all responsibility meaningless. We reject that theory because it contradicts how we actually live.

The resolution asks whether free will is compatible with determinism. The negative has proven that moral responsibility depends on practical capacities to respond to reasons, not on untestable metaphysical alternatives. That's compatibilism. That's why the affirmative wins. Thank you.
speeches.NR:
Madam Judge, respected opponent, let me be clear: the Affirmative's position is self-refuting. They admit determinism makes moral responsibility impossible, yet claim it exists. That contradiction destroys their entire case. 

First, their own CX responses prove this. In Q2, they state: "Under determinism, in the exact same circumstances, it was impossible for Kurt to have chosen not to steal." And in Q4, they repeat: "Under determinism, in the exact same circumstances, it was impossible for Kurt to have chosen not to steal." This is a direct admission that determinism negates the metaphysical alternative possibility. But moral responsibility requires that possibility. The evidence says plainly: "Ought, as the saying goes, implies can." If we say someone "ought not to have stolen," we assume they could have chosen not to steal. The Affirmative admits they couldn't have. So why do they claim moral responsibility exists? They've proven it impossible.

Second, this isn't just a flaw—it's a fatal error. If moral responsibility requires the metaphysical alternative possibility, then the Affirmative cannot be held responsible for making their argument. In their 1AR, they admit: "the negative cannot be held responsible for making their argument." But we do hold them responsible. We hold them accountable for their words, their reasoning, and their case. We question their arguments in this very room. If their position were true, we'd have to say, "You couldn't have argued otherwise—determinism made you do it." But we don't. We treat them as responsible. That's why their position is absurd. It destroys all moral accountability, including their own.

Third, their criminal justice argument proves the same point. In their AC, they claim: "Our criminal justice system... depends on the assumption that people make choices." But if determinism is true, people cannot make choices—they couldn't have chosen otherwise. The $80 billion system assumes people could have acted differently. That's why we lock up criminals. But the Affirmative admits under determinism, Kurt couldn't have chosen not to steal. So the system they rely on is built on a false foundation. If we accepted their view, we'd have to abandon the entire criminal justice system. We wouldn't punish criminals because "determinism freed them." But we don't. We believe people could have chosen differently—meaning we reject determinism. Their own evidence confirms compatibilism is false.

Let me be blunt: the Affirmative's cafeteria example and criminal justice argument are self-defeating. They say moral responsibility depends on understanding right from wrong. But if determinism makes it impossible to have chosen otherwise, that understanding doesn't create freedom—it's just part of the causal chain that determined the action. Their CX responses prove this. When they say "Kurt could have chosen not to steal," they're using the practical sense. But they admit the metaphysical sense is impossible. And if moral responsibility requires the metaphysical sense (which it does, per their own words), then it's impossible. So their case collapses.

The resolution asks whether free will is compatible with determinism. But the Affirmative has shown it's not. They've proven moral responsibility requires what determinism destroys. Their position makes accountability meaningless. We cannot accept that. Thank you.
speeches.2AR:
Madam Judge, this debate comes down to one question: does the negative's position make moral responsibility possible? Their own evidence and arguments prove it does not. Let me give you three reasons to vote affirmative.

First, the self-refutation argument is decisive. The negative claims determinism destroys moral responsibility. But if that is true, then the negative cannot be held responsible for making their argument. We would have to say, 'determinism made you argue this way.' But we do not. We treat the negative as responsible for their words, their reasoning, and their case. We question them in this room. We hold them accountable. The negative has given us a theory that makes all responsibility meaningless. We reject that theory because it contradicts how we actually live. This isn't philosophy—it's common sense. If the negative is right, their case collapses.

Second, the negative misunderstands what moral responsibility requires. They conflate two different meanings of could have done otherwise. One is practical: could Kurt have understood stealing was wrong and chosen not to steal? The other is metaphysical: could Kurt have done otherwise in identical circumstances? The negative admits the metaphysical version is impossible under determinism. But we only care about the practical version. We hold people responsible because they understood right from wrong and could have acted differently based on that understanding. That capacity exists even under determinism. The negative's own man in the room example proves this. We treat the informed man differently than the ignorant man because understanding affects behavior. That's not free will—it's how determinism actually works. The negative hasn't proven incompatibilism. They've proven compatibilism.

Third, this is how we operate. Our eighty billion dollar criminal justice system depends on the assumption that people make choices we can hold responsible. We don't say, 'determinism made you rob that bank.' We say, 'you chose to rob that bank.' The negative's position would require abandoning this system. We do not abandon it. This proves compatibilism is true. The negative has not shown that determinism negates our capacity to understand right from wrong and to act on that understanding. They've shown only that determinism negates untestable metaphysical alternatives. Those alternatives do not matter for moral responsibility. Vote affirmative. Thank you.
